title: Regression

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Regression; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: scikit-learn User Guide; 1.9.x target, accessed 2026-07; User Guide sections 1 and 3: supervised estimators, validation, thresholding, metrics, and learning curves; atomic topic: Regression
sources.claim: Regression estimates a continuous target from examples and is evaluated against simple baselines using metrics whose scale and loss shape match the decision, plus residual analysis across relevant slices.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Regression estimates a continuous target from examples and is evaluated against simple baselines using metrics whose scale and loss shape match the decision, plus residual analysis across relevant slices.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A median baseline and linear model are compared with MAE, while residuals are plotted by target range and region before any deployment claim. Your goal is to train and evaluate a baseline and linear regression model with justified metrics. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Data and experimental workflow; Conditional probability and Bayes rule; Train, validation, test and leakage. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: Regression estimates a continuous target from examples and is evaluated against simple baselines using metrics whose scale and loss shape match the decision, plus residual analysis across relevant slices. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: Low average error can hide subgroup or tail failures, extrapolation is fragile, correlation is not causation, squared loss emphasizes large errors, and target noise limits attainable performance. The learner must reject this overclaim: A positive R-squared or lower RMSE proves accurate predictions for every case and explains which features cause the target.

includes: Model continuous targets and inspect residual patterns and scale-sensitive errors.
Train and evaluate a baseline and linear regression model with justified metrics.
excludes: Low average error can hide subgroup or tail failures, extrapolation is fragile, correlation is not causation, squared loss emphasizes large errors, and target noise limits attainable performance.

outcomes: Train and evaluate a baseline and linear regression model with justified metrics.

examples.title: Regression: bounded direct case
examples.situation: A median baseline and linear model are compared with MAE, while residuals are plotted by target range and region before any deployment claim.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: A median baseline and linear model are compared with MAE, while residuals are plotted by target range and region before any deployment claim.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Compare a baseline with decision-relevant error metrics, uncertainty, residual patterns, and slices; predictive association does not establish causation.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Compare a baseline with decision-relevant error metrics, uncertainty, residual patterns, and slices; predictive association does not establish causation.

examples.title: Regression: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: A lower RMSE model is rejected for one decision because its rare underestimates exceed a safety tolerance despite better average fit.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: A lower RMSE model is rejected for one decision because its rare underestimates exceed a safety tolerance despite better average fit.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: Low average error can hide subgroup or tail failures, extrapolation is fragile, correlation is not causation, squared loss emphasizes large errors, and target noise limits attainable performance.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: A positive R-squared or lower RMSE proves accurate predictions for every case and explains which features cause the target.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Train and evaluate a baseline and linear regression model with justified metrics.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: A positive R-squared or lower RMSE proves accurate predictions for every case and explains which features cause the target.

analogy.example: a measured prediction workshop that compares a simple baseline with several fitted rules on held-out outcomes can introduce Regression only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.

limitations: prediction evidence does not establish causation, fairness, or unchanged performance after deployment

recap.summary: You should now be able to train and evaluate a baseline and linear regression model with justified metrics. Keep this boundary in view: Low average error can hide subgroup or tail failures, extrapolation is fragile, correlation is not causation, squared loss emphasizes large errors, and target noise limits attainable performance.
